captions.text: ① ROLES — they only work if they conflict
② SHARED THREAD — that’s what makes it a debate
③ TURN-TAKING — and a clean seat for a human
④ TERMINATION + SYNTHESIS — debates need to end

lines.text: Every implementation has the same four building blocks.

First, roles. An Advocate who makes the case. A Critic who attacks assumptions. A Pragmatist who represents the team that has to build and run the thing. The roles only work if they conflict. Three agents that all "analyze helpfully" are just one agent, three times, slower.

Second, a shared message thread. Everyone reads and writes the same history. The Critic sees exactly what the Advocate claimed and can quote it. That’s what makes it a debate instead of a set of parallel monologues.

Third, turn-taking. Simplest version: round-robin. Smarter version: a moderator that picks the next speaker based on where the discussion is. And here’s a nice bonus: the moderator can also call on a human. When the debate hits a question only your team can answer, it pauses, you answer into the thread, and the agents continue with real context instead of assumptions.
Fourth, termination and synthesis. Debates need to end. Round limit, convergence, or a moderator calling it. Then a judge reads the whole thread and writes the actual deliverable.
